retrieved_docs.content: Cord width ranges from 0.64 to 0.83 cm in the thoracic region to 1.27 to 1.33 cm in the cervical and lumbar regions. A 2016 systematic review of the literature demonstrated that, on average, the largest transverse diameter corresponded to the C5 neuronal segment (1.33 +/- 0.22 cm), and the smallest transverse diameter, on average, corresponded to the T8 segment (0.83 +/- 0.21 cm) [3] Enlarged regions of gray matter correspond to nerve root distribution to the upper and lower extremities.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord -- Structure and Function -- Transverse Section of the Spinal Cord
retrieved_docs.content: On sectioning a spinal cord, two distinct areas are evident with the naked eyes, an inner grey matter, and an outer white matter. The grey matter indicates the collection of cell bodies of neurons. The white matter is the collection of the fibers running through the spinal cord.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord Morphology -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The vertebral column comprises seven cervical, twelve thoracic, and five lumbar segments. In adults, the cord terminates at the level of L1 to L2. Thus, the cord spans within 20 bony vertebrae. In a child, it terminates at the upper border of L3. Each of these segments innervates a dermatome. The spinal cord consists of both white matter and gray matter. The white matter becomes sparse towards the end, and the gray matter converges to form the conus medullaris. The cord is anchored at the caudal end to the coccyx by the filum terminal, an extension of the pia mater. The spinal nerves L2 to S1 comprise the cauda equina within the subarachnoid space called the lumbar cistern. The spinal cord has two significant enlargements at the cervical and lumbar regions for brachial and lumbosacral plexus. In the thoracic region, the width of the spinal cord ranges between 0.64 to 0.83 cm, and in the cervical and lumbar regions, it ranges between 1.27 to 1.33 cm. The segment at C5 has the largest transverse diameter with 13.3 +/- 2.2 mm, which decreases to 8.3 +/- 2.1 mm at T8 and increases to 9.4 +/- 1.5 mm at L3. There is less variation in the anteroposterior diameter. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord -- Structure and Function -- Transverse Section of the Spinal Cord
retrieved_docs.content: There is variation in the amount of the areas occupied by the white and grey matter in different spinal cord levels. The space occupied by the grey matter increases progressively, and the region for white matter decreases as we go craniocaudally.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Structure and Function -- Spinal cord
retrieved_docs.content: The anatomic course originates in the brainstem before coursing through the foramen magnum.  The spinal cord continues distally through the cervical and thoracic regions of the spinal column before terminating as a tapered structure known as the conus medullaris

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord Morphology -- Structure and Function -- Cross-section of the Spinal Cord
retrieved_docs.content: Both white matter and gray matter comprise the spinal cord. The gray matter is a collection of cell bodies, and the white matter is a collection of axons. The amount of gray matter is scarce at the thoracic levels as opposed to the cervical and lumbosacral segments. The spinal cord has fissures and sulci. The anterior median fissure is central, and the anterior white commissure is at its base. The posterior median sulcus is present posteriorly, and the posterolateral sulcus is present on either side of it. The anterior nerve roots exit at the anterolateral sulcus. The posterior nerve roots exit at the posterolateral sulcus. [1] The central canal runs between the two halves of the gray matter. It is continuous with the fourth ventricle and contains cerebrospinal fluid.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord Morphology -- Structure and Function -- Central Zone- Lamina X
retrieved_docs.content: Lamina X is located centrally around the central canal. They consist of axons that cross to the opposite side of the cord. [1] [3] [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: How to Localize Neurologic Lesions by Physical Examination -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Medulla oblongata passes through the foramen magnum and continues as the spinal cord. The spinal cord has multiple segments. Each segment gives away a pair of spinal nerves. There are 8 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral, and 2 coccygeal segments. Looking at the cross-section, the spinal cord has a central gray matter that contains cell bodies of spinal nuclei and peripheral white matter that contains myelinated axons. The anterior horn of the gray matter contains spinal motor nuclei; axons of these neurons innervate skeletal muscles and are called lower motor neurons. Axons of these neurons travel through the anterior root of the spinal nerves and form brachial and lumbosacral plexuses that innervate the upper and lower limbs, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Structure and Function -- Spinal cord
retrieved_docs.content: The spinal cord's central canal is an extension of the 4th ventricle. It contains CSF; surrounding it are the gray commissure and the anterior white commissure. Decussation of the tracts of the white columns occurs at the anterior white commissure.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord -- Structure and Function -- Transverse Section of the Spinal Cord
retrieved_docs.content: Grey Matter : When taking a transverse section of the spinal cord, in the middle is the grey matter, which appears roughly in the form of an H (butterfly) shaped mass. The grey matter is divisible on each half of the cord into a larger ventral mass – ventral grey column (anterior/ventral horn) and an elongated dorsal grey column (posterior/dorsal horn) . In some parts of the cord (thoracic and upper lumbar regions), a small lateral projection of a grey mater is present between the ventral and dorsal columns. This structure is a lateral grey column (lateral horn) . The cells in the anterior horn are called anterior horn cells.  The grey matter on both the left and right halves connect by the grey commissure, which contains the central canal. This canal communicates above into the fourth ventricle. The central canal expands at the lower end in the conus medullaris to form the terminal ventricle. The central canal contains CSF and is lined by ependymal cells. [5] For experimental purposes, the grey matter divides into ten laminae. The laminae I to VI occupy the posterior grey column. [6] The lamina VII is in the intermediate region, lamina VIII remains confined to the medial part of the anterior grey column, and lamina IX occupies the lateral part of the anterior grey column. The area around the central canal is distinguished as lamina X. [7] Each of these laminae has unique connections and functions.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord -- Structure and Function -- Covering of Spinal Cord
retrieved_docs.content: The spinal cord's most important function is to receive, integrate, associate the input, and produce a reflex response.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatic injuries of the spinal cord: It is the commonest, accounting for almost 90% of all spinal cord injuries. It frequently results from road traffic accidents, falls, and sports injuries. They can have devastating effects on the life of a person. Lesions in the lower thoracic region lead to paraplegia, and that in the cervical area leads to quadriplegia. [28] Compression: It can be due to intervertebral disc herniation or the vertebras' dislocation leading to compression of the spinal cord. Symptoms due to compression can be paresis to paralysis. Hemisection: Traumatic injuries can lead to an incomplete section of a part of the spinal cord. A classical presentation of a hemisection is the Brown-Sequard syndrome . There is ipsilateral motor loss below the section in this syndrome, contralateral loss of pain, and temperature sensations, with no loss of ipsilateral light touch sensations. Complete section: It is a condition where there is the absence of sacral sparing with no sensation in the segment of S4-5 or the lack of voluntary contraction of the anal sphincter. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Spinal Cord -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The spinal cord is an integral aspect of the central nervous system, which is cylindrical and elongated in structure. It is one of the most important contents of the vertebral canal. It presents as an extension of the lower part of the brain stem, i.e., the medulla oblongata. It begins at the first cervical vertebra level and ends at the first lumbar vertebra in an adult. [1] But in the case of young children, the spinal cord is relatively longer relative to the length of the vertebral column. Here it usually ends much lower at the level of the upper border of the third lumbar vertebrae.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Structure and Function -- Spinal cord
retrieved_docs.content: The spinal cord proper terminates at the L1-L2 vertebral level and is anchored distally via the filum terminale, representing an extension of the pia mater with fibrous attachments to the coccyx

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Transmission imaging is linked to صورة مقطعية محوسبة, indicating that this imaging modality encompasses computed tomography scans. In clinical practice, صورة مقطعية محوسبة frequently co‑occurs with صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية, reflecting that CT imaging is often performed together with spinal‑region myelographic or spinal cord imaging to provide complementary anatomical detail for diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2307
retrieved_docs.content: The central nervous system (centrální nervový systém) is closely associated with the dura mater, reflecting an intimate anatomical relationship in which the dura mater envelops the CNS. The CNS is identified as the location where injuries to the central nervous system (إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي) are observed. The dura mater is specifically noted as the finding site of تمزق جافوي, a distinct lesion. This lesion, تمزق جافوي, is classified as a type of central nervous system injury (إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي) and is also regarded as the parent condition for spinal cord injury, indicating that spinal cord injury can be considered a subcategory or consequence of تمزق جافوي. Together, these relationships illustrate how the CNS, its protective covering, and related injuries such as تمزق جافوي and spinal cord injury are interconnected, emphasizing the importance of recognizing these links for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: The condition تمزق جافوي is closely associated with the dura mater, as the dura mater serves as the primary site where findings of تمزق جافوي are observed. In addition, تمزق جافوي functions as an antecedent or parent condition for spinal cord injury, indicating that the presence of تمزق جافوي may predispose patients to subsequent spinal cord injury. Recognizing the involvement of the dura mater in تمزق جافوي and its potential role in the development of spinal cord injury is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: The central nervous system (centrální nervový systém) is described as a physical component of the dura mater, indicating its anatomical relationship within the meningeal coverings. In clinical assessment, the central nervous system serves as the finding site for central nervous system injury (إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي), highlighting its relevance as the location where such pathological findings are identified. Understanding that the central nervous system is both anatomically linked to the dura mater and is the primary site of CNS injuries informs diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in neurology and neurosurgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Injury of central nervous system due to birth trauma is a condition in which the pathological finding is located within the central nervous system. The central nervous system is anatomically linked to the dura mater, as the dura mater constitutes a physical component of the protective meningeal layers that surround the brain and spinal cord. Consequently, injuries to the central nervous system, such as those caused by birth trauma, involve structures that are encased by the dura mater, underscoring the importance of the dura mater in shielding the central nervous system and influencing clinical assessment and management of such injuries.

Consequently, a cervical spine fracture that involves the spinal cord can lead to direct tissue injury that may produce neurotoxic effects, highlighting the importance of addressing both structural damage and its potential neurotoxic consequences in patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1652
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion and the injury whose intent is undetermined but resulted from an aircraft crash are both described as having an associated morphology of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). The presence of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage) is further linked to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), indicating that the tissue damage observed in these severe injuries may involve neurotoxic processes. This interconnected understanding highlights that both the cervical spine fracture with cord involvement and the ambiguous aircraft‑related injury share a common pathological feature of tissue damage, which can contribute to neurotoxic effects that are relevant for diagnosis, management, and prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2896
retrieved_docs.content: تمزق جافوي is classified as a type of إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي (central nervous system injury). Within this hierarchy, spinal cord injury is considered a subcategory of تمزق جافوي, indicating that spinal cord injury falls under the broader category of تمزق جافوي and, consequently, is part of the spectrum of central nervous system injuries. This relationship clarifies that spinal cord injury is a specific manifestation within the larger group of injuries represented by تمزق جافوي, which itself is recognized as a central nervous system injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 604
retrieved_docs.content: External cause codes: Firearm are classified as Injury due to legal intervention by firearm. Injury due to legal intervention by firearm is associated with the morphology of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion also has the associated morphology تَلَف الأنسجة, indicating that tissue damage is a common pathological feature of both firearm‑related legal intervention injuries and cervical spine fractures with spinal cord involvement. The tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة) is further linked to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), suggesting that the morphological consequence of tissue injury may involve neurotoxic processes. Recognizing these connections helps clinicians understand injury mechanisms, anticipate tissue and neural complications, and guide appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion is classified as a كسر في الفقرة الرقبية (cervical vertebral fracture). In addition, كسر الحوض (pelvic fracture) frequently co‑occurs with كسر في الفقرة الرقبية, indicating that patients who sustain a cervical vertebral fracture often have concurrent pelvic injuries. This interrelationship underscores the importance of comprehensive assessment for both cervical and pelvic trauma in affected individuals.
